content.text: PR [#769](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/769) is open. Per the working agreement (review agents on every PR), running a proportionate low-effort review pass:

result.output: Launching skill: code-review

content.text: (Re-invocation of /code-review — the skill instructions were previously loaded; the arguments or dynamic output below are new.)

Review target: `769 low`

`high effort → 3+5 angles × 6 candidates → 1-vote verify (recall-biased) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** via the Agent tool. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates bypass the verify step and are the
dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Verify (1-vote, recall-biased)

Dedup near-duplicates (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). For
each remaining candidate, run **one verifier** via the Agent tool:
give it the diff, the relevant file(s), and the candidate; it returns exactly
one of **CONFIRMED / PLAUSIBLE / REFUTED**.

**PLAUSIBLE by default** — do not refute a candidate for being "speculative" or
"depends on runtime state" when the state is realistic: concurrency races,
nil/undefined on a rare-but-reachable path (error handler, cold cache, missing
optional field), falsy-zero treated as missing, off-by-one on a boundary the
code does not exclude, retry storms / partial failures, regex/allowlist that
lost an anchor. These are PLAUSIBLE.

**REFUTED** only when constructible from the code: factually wrong (quote the
actual line); provably impossible (type/constant/invariant — show it); already
handled in this diff (cite the guard); or pure style with no observable effect.

Keep **CONFIRMED and PLAUSIBLE**. Drop REFUTED.

## Output

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives verification, return `[]`.


## Posting to GitHub (--comment)

The `--comment` flag was passed. After producing the findings list, if the
review target is a GitHub PR, post each finding as an inline PR comment via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` (one call per finding;
include a suggestion block only when it fully fixes the issue). If that tool
is not available in this session, fall back to `gh api` (repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments)
or print the findings instead. If the target is not a PR, print the findings
to the terminal and note that `--comment` was ignored.

content.text:
## Asset details — all 5 datasources; lineage edges — dbt-fed only; "view" — nobody yet

**Asset identity (the `assets` entity, #757):** all five datasources get OpenLineage-named asset rows, stamped on every run — Snowflake, Unity Catalog, ADLS Gen2 and S3 (flat-file pattern → base-prefix asset), and Iceberg (`catalog_uri` namespace). Orchestration connections correctly have no asset identity. So "asset details" coverage is complete at the data layer.

**Lineage edges (`lineage_edges`, #759):** these come from exactly one source today — **dbt `manifest.json`**. That means lineage exists only for tables a dbt project touches. In practice that's Snowflake (proven live yesterday, including your add/drop-view scenario); the parser is adapter-aware, so a dbt project on Databricks/UC would also feed edges, but that path hasn't been exercised live. What has **no lineage feed at all** right now: flat-file (ADLS/S3), Iceberg, and any warehouse relationship not expressed in dbt (hand-built views, BI reports, engine-native lineage). Separately, OL emission (#758) sends run/dataset events *outbound* to a receiver like Marquez — that's DataQ telling the world, not DataQ ingesting lineage.

**Lineage *view*:** the honest answer is **no datasource has one yet**. `downstream_assets`/`upstream_assets` is a service-layer query with no API endpoint and no UI — yesterday's test had to call it from inside the worker container. That's the very next issue.

## What's left in the G-d program

| Issue | What it delivers | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **#760 — asset view** | The read surface: `/assets` API + assets page/UI, blast-radius (up/downstream) exposed per asset, `asset_id` on SuiteRead/RunRead (deferred there from #764 review), grant-derived authz | **Next up** |
| **#761 — incidents** | Incident entity anchored to `(asset_id, check_id)`, evidence card, `ResultPublisher` delivery, reopen-as-new-linked | Not started |
| **#762 — LineageProvider seam + Marquez pull** | Ingesting lineage from a catalog/OL backend (in-repo compose Marquez as reference) — this is what closes the gaps above: UC engine-native lineage, and your **Power BI report scenario** (BI nodes only ever arrive via catalog pull — Purview/DataHub — with the "downstream nodes aren't always tables" seam contract recorded on the issue) | Not started |
| Nav inversion | Assets become a first-class nav concept (files after #760 lands) | To file |

Two smaller items now attached to the program from yesterday's testing: **#767** (P1, per-check result misattribution when a check errors — worth fixing before incident evidence cards trust per-check history) and the orphan-asset sweep (asset rows accrete by design with frozen `last_seen`; the sweep itself is future work per ADR 0034).

Want me to start #760?

Yes, plan out for all the issues [760, 761, 762, Nav Inversion, 767 & orphan-asset sweep] then start the work based on priority and dependency. 
you can be the orchestrator and deligate the task to opus/sonet/haiku.
Let me pull the issue ACs quickly so the plan matches what's already specced, then I'll lay out the order and start delegating.

input.description: Fix #767 result misattribution
input.prompt:
Fix GitHub issue #767 in DataQ: per-check results get attributed to the wrong check_id when any check errors. You are in an isolated git worktree of /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ — create branch `fix/issue-767-result-attribution` and commit there. Do NOT touch the user's running docker stack or the main checkout.

## The bug (verified live on both Snowflake and UC, 2026-07-10)
- GX 1.17 `Validator.graph_validate` returns results in a DIFFERENT order when any expectation errors: errored expectations are appended first, then the remaining pass/fail results in original order.
- `backend/app/datasources/gx_runner.py` `to_suite_outcome()` copies `gx_result.results` order verbatim.
- `backend/app/services/run_service.py` `_run_all_checks` (~L138) and the persistence zip (~L211) map outcomes back to checks POSITIONALLY (`zip(..., strict=True)`) — the docstring even states the order contract.
- Net: with ≥2 expectation checks where one errors (e.g. its column was dropped), result rows are stamped with the wrong check_id. Read the full issue: `gh issue view 767 -R TheurgicDuke771/DataQ` (and its comment with the Snowflake repro).

## Fix direction (validate, then implement the most robust)
Preferred: inject a positional marker into each expectation's `meta` when gx_runner builds the GX suite from `CheckSpec`s (e.g. `meta={"dataq_index": i}`), then have `to_suite_outcome` re-sort/re-key results by that marker (GX carries `expectation_config.meta` through to each result). This is duplicate-safe (two checks with identical type+kwargs stay distinguishable) — verify GX 1.17 actually propagates meta on the installed version (conda env `dataq`); if it doesn't, fall back to multiset matching on (expectation_type, kwargs) with stable tie-breaking, and say so. Keep the fix inside the GX translation layer (gx_runner) so `run_service`'s order contract becomes actually guaranteed rather than assumed — do NOT re-architect run_service. Check ALL runner paths that feed to_suite_outcome (snowflake.py, flatfile.py, unity_catalog.py, iceberg.py, gx_runner shared helpers) so every datasource gets the marker.

## Requirements
- Follow repo conventions (CLAUDE.md, CONTRIBUTING): Black, Ruff, mypy-clean incl. backend/tests, no new deps.
- **Regression test that fails on current main**: ≥2 expectation checks where one errors (pandas/in-memory GX path — existing gx_runner/flat-file tests show the pattern; no live credentials). Assert each outcome maps to the submitted spec (e.g. by expectation_type/kwargs). Also cover: all-pass order preserved; duplicate identical expectations; error-first reordering explicitly simulated if the real GX path is hard to force (but DO try forcing a real error via a missing column in a pandas DataFrame — that's exactly the live repro).
- Run locally in the worktree: `conda run -n dataq black --check backend/`, `ruff check backend/`, `mypy backend/app backend/tests` (match pyproject config), and the targeted pytest for touched test modules plus `backend/tests/datasources/` and `backend/tests/services/` (use `-o addopts=""` to skip the coverage gate on partial runs). Everything green.
- Conventional commit(s) on the branch, e.g. `fix(gx): re-key GX results to submitted checks — order breaks when an expectation errors (Fixes #767)`. Do NOT push, do NOT open a PR — the orchestrator does that.

## Deliverable (final message)
Branch name, files changed with a 1-paragraph description of the mechanism chosen (meta-marker vs matching, and the GX propagation evidence), test names added + red/green proof (show the regression test failing against the pre-fix code — e.g. `git stash` the fix or run against main's version — then passing), gate results, and any caveats.

Existing patterns: backend/app/api/v1/ routers + authz deps (suite-scoped authz, ADR 0027 — workspace-admin implicit admin + workspace-wide visibility; 404-no-leak discipline), backend/app/db/models.py (Asset, LineageEdge, Suite.asset_id, Run.asset_id), backend/app/lineage/edges.py (downstream_assets/upstream_assets BFS), backend/app/services/ service-layer style, frontend/src/ page/nav/AsyncBody/useMe patterns and frontend tests (Vitest + RTL; antd v6 — `title` not `message` on Alert).

## Scope (from the issue)
Backend:
- `GET /api/v1/assets` — list assets visible to the caller. **Authz derived, never granted**: an asset is visible iff the caller can view ≥1 suite targeting it (suites.asset_id); workspace-admin sees all. Include per-asset aggregation: composing suites (filtered to caller's grants), latest run/check outcome summary, env, namespace/name, last_seen.
- `GET /api/v1/assets/{id}` — detail: the aggregation above plus **upstream/downstream lineage** (the edges BFS, both directions, depth-capped as the service supports) with each node carrying enough to render (namespace, name, whether it's monitored i.e. has suites). 404-no-leak for assets outside the caller's grants.
- Asset-metadata mutation (owner/description) is workspace-Admin-only per the issue — check whether Asset has owner/description columns; if they don't exist yet, add them via ONE additive migration (backward-compatible, nullable) + a `PATCH /assets/{id}` admin-gated endpoint. Keep it minimal.
- Expose `asset_id` on SuiteRead and RunRead schemas (deferred to #760 by the #764 review — search for that TODO/comment).
- MCP parity: DO NOT add an MCP tool; record "explicit defer" in your report (the AC requires the decision recorded — the orchestrator will note it on the issue).
- Note on roles: ADR 0033 (Viewer role) is NOT built yet (#740–#743 open) — the authz matrix covers what exists today: owner / edit-share / view-share / no-share / workspace-admin. State Viewer as N/A-until-#741 in tests/comments.

Frontend:
- Assets list page (sidebar nav item "Assets" — NO navigation inversion, that's a later phase): table of visible assets (name, namespace chip/env, suites count, latest health, last_seen), click-through to detail.
- Asset detail page: identity header, health-across-suites section (the AC demands rendering health across ≥2 suites targeting the same asset — the demo seed may need a second suite on one asset; check the seed and extend it if that's the established pattern), upstream/downstream lineage lists (simple lists/trees, no graph viz needed), links to suites/runs.
- Follow existing routing/layout/error-page conventions; add Playwright spec(s) mirroring frontend/e2e patterns if that's how sibling pages are covered.

## Requirements
- ≥80% coverage on new modules (the CI gate is real). Authz matrix test is MANDATORY (owner/edit/view/no-share/workspace-admin + partial-grant aggregation filtering + 404-no-leak).
- Backward-compatible migration only (if you add columns); test up+down+up against a throwaway postgres (docker run a scratch pg or sqlite-alembic if that's the repo pattern — check existing migration tests, e.g. tests/services/test_asset_identity_migration_parity.py).
- Local gate in the worktree before finishing: `conda run -n dataq black --check backend/`, `ruff check backend/`, `mypy backend/app backend/tests`, `bandit -c pyproject.toml -r backend/app`, full backend pytest (`conda run -n dataq pytest backend/tests` — the coverage gate must pass), and frontend: `pnpm install` (fresh worktree), `pnpm lint`, `pnpm test` (and `pnpm test:coverage` if feasible). Everything green.
- Conventional commits on the branch (`feat(assets): …`), one functionality per commit where practical. Do NOT push, do NOT open a PR — the orchestrator does that.
- HEADS-UP: a sibling agent is concurrently fixing #767 in backend/app/datasources/gx_runner.py + run_service zip sites — do not modify those files; your work should not need them.

## Deliverable (final message)
Branch name; endpoint list with authz semantics; migration id (if any); files changed grouped backend/frontend; test inventory (esp. the authz matrix) + coverage numbers for new modules; gate results (each command, pass/fail); the demo-seed change if made; screenshots N/A (no running stack) — describe the UI structure instead; caveats/decisions (e.g. owner/description columns added or deferred, MCP defer note).

  --body "G-d follow-up (ADR 0034: asset rows accrete; \`last_seen\` + a **sweep**, not deletes, is the cleanup posture). Observed live in the 2026-07-10 lineage verification: after a dbt model/view was removed, its \`lineage_edges\` were pruned correctly but the \`assets\` row remains with \`last_seen\` frozen at the last refresh that saw it — by design, and the frozen \`last_seen\` is exactly the sweep signal.

## Scope
- A periodic beat task (join the existing janitor cadence — cf. the stuck-run reaper / retention sweep patterns) that deletes \`assets\` rows where ALL of:
  - \`last_seen\` older than a configurable threshold (Settings-typed, e.g. \`ASSET_ORPHAN_RETENTION_DAYS\`, generous default);
  - no \`suites.asset_id\` reference; no \`runs.asset_id\` reference; no \`lineage_edges\` reference (either end);
  - (forward-compat) no \`incidents\` reference once #761 lands — write the guard so a new referencing table is an explicit checklist item, not a silent miss.
- Fail-soft, chunked deletes, structured log of count swept.
- Unit tests: referenced-asset never swept (each reference kind), threshold boundary, empty sweep.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Sweep never deletes an asset referenced by suites/runs/lineage_edges (and #761 incidents if merged first)
- [ ] Threshold configurable via Settings; default documented in .env.app.example
- [ ] Beat wiring follows the existing janitor pattern; ≥80% coverage on the new module

## Summary

**Branch:** `fix/issue-767-result-attribution` (commit `d709f9f9`, not pushed)

### Mechanism chosen: positional meta-marker (the preferred approach)
I verified empirically against the installed GX 1.17.2 (conda env `dataq`) that (a) the error-first reorder is real — submitting `[id, nope(errors), amt]` returns `[nope, id, amt]` — and (b) `meta={"dataq_index": i}` **propagates verbatim** onto each result's `expectation_config.meta` and stays out of `.kwargs` (so it never leaks into `expected_value`). Both facts confirmed, so the meta-marker path (not multiset matching) is used. It is duplicate-safe: two checks with identical `expectation_type`+`kwargs` get distinct indices and stay 1:1.

### Files changed
- **`backend/app/datasources/gx_runner.py`** — the whole fix lives in the shared GX translation layer, so every runner (snowflake/flatfile/unity_catalog/iceberg — all route through `run_expectations` → `_execute`) is covered centrally:
  - `_to_gx_expectation(spec, index=None)` gains an optional `index`; when set it stamps `meta={"dataq_index": i}` (merged with any caller meta). Default-None preserves the existing re-exported/test callers.
  - `_execute` builds the suite with `_to_gx_expectation(check, index=i)` per check.
  - New `_submission_index` / `_in_submission_order` helpers; `to_suite_outcome` now iterates results **re-sorted by the marker**, falling back to GX's list order if any result lacks a marker (keeps constructed unit-test results working). `run_service` is untouched — its positional-zip order contract is now actually guaranteed instead of assumed.
- **`backend/tests/datasources/test_flatfile.py`** — 2 real-GX end-to-end regression tests (pandas path, real missing-column error): the errored check submitted **last** so the reorder actively breaks positional mapping; plus a duplicate-identical-expectations test.
- **`backend/tests/datasources/test_gx_runner.py`** — 3 unit tests on `to_suite_outcome`: explicit error-first reorder simulation, all-pass order preserved, and no-marker fallback.

### Red/green proof
- **Red:** stashing only the source fix (tests intact), the 3 new attribution tests **failed** against pre-fix code — e.g. `test_run_checks_errored_check_maps_to_its_own_spec_despite_gx_reorder` and `test_to_suite_outcome_reorders_errored_first_gx_result`, with the errored "column does not exist" content landing on the wrong outcome position (the exact live cross-wiring).
- **Green:** with the fix restored, all 5 new tests pass; the full `backend/tests/datasources/` + `backend/tests/services/` run is **715 passed, 135 skipped** (skips are the deferred live-datasource network seams).

### Gate results (all green, in worktree)
- `black --check backend/` — all 250 files unchanged
- `ruff check backend/` — all checks passed
- `mypy backend/app backend/tests` — no issues in 221 files
- `bandit -r gx_runner.py` — clean

### Caveats
- The two lower-severity items the issue notes as related are **out of scope and left as-is** (as the issue frames them): `checks_total` deliberately excluding `error`/`skip` tiers (by design in `check_outcome_counts`), and `observed_value.error` surfacing the raw GX exception string (no DataQ-normalized reason code). Neither is an attribution bug; file separately if desired.
- Per the task, not pushed and no PR opened — the orchestrator handles that.</result>

- \`to_suite_outcome\` re-sorts results by the marker (fallback to list order when unmarked, keeping constructed-result tests valid). \`run_service\`'s positional contract is now guaranteed instead of assumed; all four runners are covered centrally via the shared \`_execute\`.
- Duplicate-safe: identical type+kwargs checks keep distinct indices.

## Tests (red/green proven — new attribution tests fail with the fix stashed)
- Real-GX pandas E2E: errored check submitted LAST so GX's reorder actively breaks positional mapping; duplicate-identical-expectations case.

content.text: (Re-invocation of /code-review — the skill instructions were previously loaded; the arguments or dynamic output below are new.)

Review target: `771 medium`

`high effort → 3+5 angles × 6 candidates → 1-vote verify (recall-biased) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** via the Agent tool. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates bypass the verify step and are the
dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Verify (1-vote, recall-biased)

Dedup near-duplicates (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). For
each remaining candidate, run **one verifier** via the Agent tool:
give it the diff, the relevant file(s), and the candidate; it returns exactly
one of **CONFIRMED / PLAUSIBLE / REFUTED**.

**PLAUSIBLE by default** — do not refute a candidate for being "speculative" or
"depends on runtime state" when the state is realistic: concurrency races,
nil/undefined on a rare-but-reachable path (error handler, cold cache, missing
optional field), falsy-zero treated as missing, off-by-one on a boundary the
code does not exclude, retry storms / partial failures, regex/allowlist that
lost an anchor. These are PLAUSIBLE.

**REFUTED** only when constructible from the code: factually wrong (quote the
actual line); provably impossible (type/constant/invariant — show it); already
handled in this diff (cite the guard); or pure style with no observable effect.

Keep **CONFIRMED and PLAUSIBLE**. Drop REFUTED.

## Output

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives verification, return `[]`.


## Posting to GitHub (--comment)

The `--comment` flag was passed. After producing the findings list, if the
review target is a GitHub PR, post each finding as an inline PR comment via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` (one call per finding;
include a suggestion block only when it fully fixes the issue). If that tool
is not available in this session, fall back to `gh api` (repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments)
or print the findings instead. If the target is not a PR, print the findings
to the terminal and note that `--comment` was ignored.
Running the medium-effort review on PR #771 — three finder angles in parallel (line-by-line, cross-file tracer, cleanup/conventions):

"""
    return "".join(part.title() for part in expectation_type.split("_"))


def _to_gx_expectation(spec: CheckSpec, index: int | None = None) -> Any:
    """Build the concrete GX expectation for `spec`.

    When ``index`` is given, stamp the check's submission position into the
    expectation's ``meta`` (``dataq_index``) so `to_suite_outcome` can re-key the
    result back to its spec regardless of the order GX returns results in (#767).
    Merged with any caller-supplied ``meta`` in ``kwargs``.
    """
    class_name = _expectation_class_name(spec.expectation_type)
    expectation_cls = getattr(gxe, class_name, None)
    if expectation_cls is None:
        raise UnknownExpectationError(
            f"Unknown expectation_type {spec.expectation_type!r} (no gx class {class_name!r})"
        )
    if index is None:
        return expectation_cls(**spec.kwargs)
    kwargs = dict(spec.kwargs)
    meta = dict(kwargs.get("meta") or {})
    meta[_INDEX_META_KEY] = index
    kwargs["meta"] = meta
    return expectation_cls(**kwargs)


def _is_identifier_index_list(value: Any) -> bool:
    """A useful `unexpected_index_list` is a non-empty list of **row dicts** (the
    identifier columns + failing value, from `unexpected_index_column_names`). A plain
    COMPLETE run instead returns bare positional indices (``[1, 4, …]``) — not a
    locator, so we drop those to keep the sample clean."""
    return (
        isinstance(value, list) and len(value) > 0 and all(isinstance(row, dict) for row in value)
    )


def _extract_sample_failures(result: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any] | None:
    sample: dict[str, Any] = {}
    for key in _SAMPLE_KEYS:
        if key not in result:
            continue
        if key == "unexpected_index_list" and not _is_identifier_index_list(result[key]):
            continue
        sample[key] = result[key]
    return sample or None
    ``None`` when absent (a manually-constructed / legacy result carrying no marker)."""

    meta = getattr(config, "meta", None)
    if isinstance(meta, dict):
        index = meta.get(_INDEX_META_KEY)
        if isinstance(index, int) and not isinstance(index, bool):
            return index
    return None


def _in_submission_order(results: list[Any]) -> list[Any]:
    """Re-key GX results back to submission order via the `dataq_index` marker (#767).

    GX 1.17 `graph_validate` returns results in submission order *only* while nothing
    errors; once any expectation errors it appends the errored ones first, so trusting
    list order cross-wires results to the wrong check. Sorting by the stamped index
    restores the 1:1 positional contract the run-service relies on. Falls back to GX's
    order if *any* result lacks the marker (constructed results in unit tests), so the
    legacy no-marker path is unchanged.
    """
    indexed: list[tuple[int, Any]] = []
    for result in results:
        index = _submission_index(result)
        if index is None:
            return results
        indexed.append((index, result))
    indexed.sort(key=lambda pair: pair[0])
    return [result for _, result in indexed]


def to_suite_outcome(gx_result: Any) -> SuiteOutcome:
    """Map a GX ExpectationSuiteValidationResult onto our GX-agnostic DTO.

    Results are re-keyed to submission order via the `dataq_index` marker (#767)
    before mapping, so the outcome list stays 1:1 with the submitted `CheckSpec`s
    even when GX reorders errored expectations to the front.

    Kept GX-translation-only (no datasource specifics) so it is unit-testable
    with a constructed GX result, no live datasource required.
    """
    outcomes: list[CheckOutcome] = []
    for check_result in _in_submission_order(list(gx_result.results)):
        config = check_result.expectation_config
        detail: dict[str, Any] = check_result.result or {}
        observed = (
            {"observed_value": detail["observed_value"]} if "observed_value" in detail else None

def test_to_suite_outcome_without_markers_falls_back_to_gx_order() -> None:
    # Legacy / manually-constructed results (no meta marker) keep GX's list order —
    # backward-compatible with the existing constructed-result tests.
    gx_result = SimpleNamespace(
        success=True,
        results=[
            _marked_result(index=None, type_="expect_x", kwargs={}),
            _marked_result(index=None, type_="expect_y", kwargs={}),
        ],
    )
    outcome = to_suite_outcome(gx_result)
    assert [c.expectation_type for c in outcome.checks] == ["expect_x", "expect_y"]


def test_to_suite_outcome_partial_markers_falls_back_and_warns(caplog: Any) -> None:
    # A *partial* marker loss is anomalous (every production expectation is stamped):
    # keep GX's order — never guess — but emit a warning so the fallback is visible
    # instead of silently resurrecting the #767 cross-wiring.
    import logging

    gx_result = SimpleNamespace(
        success=True,
        results=[
            _marked_result(index=1, type_="expect_x", kwargs={}),
            _marked_result(index=None, type_="expect_y", kwargs={}),
        ],
    )
    with caplog.at_level(logging.WARNING):
        outcome = to_suite_outcome(gx_result)
    assert [c.expectation_type for c in outcome.checks] == ["expect_x", "expect_y"]
    assert "gx_results_partially_unmarked" in caplog.text


def test_to_gx_expectation_non_dict_meta_surfaces_gx_error() -> None:
    # A malformed stored `meta` (legacy row) must produce GX's own validation error,
    # not a bare ValueError from the marker merge (dict("garbage")).
    import pytest

    from backend.app.datasources.base import CheckSpec
    from backend.app.datasources.gx_runner import _to_gx_expectation

    spec = CheckSpec(
        expectation_type="expect_column_values_to_not_be_null",
        kwargs={"column": "id", "meta": "garbage"},
    )
    with pytest.raises(Exception) as excinfo:
        _to_gx_expectation(spec, index=0)
    assert not isinstance(excinfo.value, (ValueError, TypeError)) or "validation" in str(
        excinfo.value
    ).lower(), f"expected GX's validation error, got bare {excinfo.value!r}"

def test_to_suite_outcome_partial_markers_falls_back_and_warns(caplog: Any) -> None:
    # A *partial* marker loss is anomalous (every production expectation is stamped):
    # keep GX's order — never guess — but emit a warning so the fallback is visible
    # instead of silently resurrecting the #767 cross-wiring.

    gx_result = SimpleNamespace(
        success=True,
        results=[
            _marked_result(index=1, type_="expect_x", kwargs={}),
            _marked_result(index=None, type_="expect_y", kwargs={}),
        ],
    )
    with caplog.at_level(logging.WARNING):
        outcome = to_suite_outcome(gx_result)
    assert [c.expectation_type for c in outcome.checks] == ["expect_x", "expect_y"]
    assert "gx_results_partially_unmarked" in caplog.text
input.new_string:
def test_to_suite_outcome_partial_markers_falls_back_and_warns() -> None:
    # A *partial* marker loss is anomalous (every production expectation is stamped):
    # keep GX's order — never guess — but emit a warning so the fallback is visible
    # instead of silently resurrecting the #767 cross-wiring.
    from structlog.testing import capture_logs

    gx_result = SimpleNamespace(
        success=True,
        results=[
            _marked_result(index=1, type_="expect_x", kwargs={}),
            _marked_result(index=None, type_="expect_y", kwargs={}),
        ],
    )
    with capture_logs() as logs:
        outcome = to_suite_outcome(gx_result)
    assert [c.expectation_type for c in outcome.checks] == ["expect_x", "expect_y"]
    assert any(entry["event"] == "gx_results_partially_unmarked" for entry in logs)

## Backend
- **\`GET /api/v1/assets\`** — assets visible via **derived authz**: an asset appears iff the caller can view ≥1 suite targeting it (workspace-admin sees all); aggregation (suite count, rolled-up health, last_seen) filtered to the caller's grants.

- **Assets list** (\`/assets\`, sidebar nav — NO navigation inversion, that's phase 4) and **Asset detail** (identity header, health across composing suites, upstream/downstream lineage panels, suite/run links).
- Demo seed gains a second suite on the shared \`ORDERS\` asset so the detail page renders health across ≥2 suites.

- New-module coverage: \`api/v1/assets.py\` 100%, \`asset_view_service.py\` 99%, \`Assets.tsx\` 100%, \`AssetDetail.tsx\` 96%.

input.prompt:
You are a code-review FINDER (angle: authz/security attacker) for DataQ PR #772 (asset view). The PR's authz contract (ADR 0027 + issue #760): an asset is visible iff the caller can view ≥1 suite targeting it; aggregation must be filtered to the caller's grants; 404-no-leak for assets wholly outside grants; workspace-admin sees all; PATCH is workspace-admin-only; lineage nodes deliberately expose only OL identity + is_monitored (never run data/grants).

Read the branch code at /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/.claude/worktrees/agent-aa5a4a63355ac5d2d/: backend/app/services/asset_view_service.py, backend/app/api/v1/assets.py, and compare against the established authz idioms in backend/app/api/v1/suites.py + runs.py + the suite-grant helpers they use (find them). Also check backend/app/api/v1/runs.py + suites.py diffs (asset_id exposure).

Hunt for: (1) info leaks — does ANY field in list/detail aggregate data from suites the caller can NOT view (latest-run outcome, health rollup, suite_count, last_seen sourced from an invisible suite's runs)? (2) 404-vs-403 leak — can an attacker distinguish "asset exists but not mine" from "asset doesn't exist" via status/body/timing? (3) does asset_id on RunRead/SuiteRead leak assets of unshared suites anywhere else (e.g. pipeline_runs, admin views, MCP tools reusing these schemas)? (4) PATCH: mass-assignment (extra fields), owner_user_id set to a nonexistent user, description size/content limits (stored XSS vector for the frontend?), missing 404-no-leak semantics on PATCH for non-admins? (5) is_monitored on lineage nodes: does it leak the existence of OTHER users' suites on unshared assets (contract says yes-by-design for structure — but confirm nothing more leaks, e.g. suite names/ids)? (6) SQL injection or unbounded-query risks in the new queries (pagination missing on /assets?).

Return up to 6 candidates as JSON: [{file, line, summary, failure_scenario}]. Concrete attacker scenarios only. No edits.

input.message:
The agentic review of your PR (#772) produced 8 findings (posted as inline comments — `gh pr view 772 --comments` / `gh api repos/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pulls/772/comments`) plus 6 test gaps. Fix ALL of them in your worktree on the same branch, then a single `fixup:` commit (do NOT amend, do NOT push — the orchestrator pushes).

Code findings:
1. **BLOCKING — migration revision-id collision**: your `b2c3d4e5f6a7` is already owned by the merged `b2c3d4e5f6a7_add_suite_notifications.py`. Generate a fresh unique revision id, rename the file to match, keep `down_revision="a1c2e3d4f5b6"`. Verify `conda run -n dataq python -m alembic heads` (from backend/) shows exactly ONE head and `alembic history` traverses clean. This is why the Playwright CI job is red.
2. **PATCH owner_user_id**: pre-check the User row exists → clean 422 (match the suite/share FK-precheck idiom), no unhandled IntegrityError.
3. **description cap**: `Field(max_length=1024)` on AssetMetadataUpdate (match SuiteCreate).
4. **GET /assets pagination**: limit/offset query params with the /runs idiom (`ge=1, le=200`, default 200 — check runs.py for exact shape) threaded into list_visible_assets.
5. **health.ts must consider run.status**: operational `failed` → an error/failing tag (not green); `queued`/`running` → don't show green "Passing" (use the run-status handling conventions from the Results/dashboard pages — read how they map status+severity and mirror it).
6. **AssetDetail: swap raw antd List → the SimpleList shim** (the #516/#693 component) for the lineage panels.
7. **updateAsset dead client**: wire a minimal admin-only edit for `description` on AssetDetail (useMe().is_workspace_admin gating pattern; small inline edit or modal; test it). Owner reassignment UI stays deferred — note that in code where natural.
8. **Orphan assets for admin**: `get_visible_asset(include_all=True)` must return suite-less assets (empty suites list) instead of 404; non-admin behavior unchanged. Test both.

Test gaps to close (from the test-quality angle):
a. 404-no-leak asserted on BODY equality (unknown id vs no-grant → identical status AND body).
b. PATCH tested through the real route with JSON bodies: explicit `null` vs omitted field semantics (model_fields_set), unknown fields rejected.
c. Malformed/garbage UUID on both GET endpoints (expect 4xx, not 500).
d. NUL-byte + oversized description via the route (the #570 guard class; expect 422).
e. Lineage BFS: cycle in lineage_edges + a node reachable both upstream and downstream (depth-cap respected, no hang).
f. Asset whose only composing suite is deleted after having runs (detail + list behavior).

Then run the FULL gate: black --check backend/, ruff check backend/, mypy backend/app backend/tests, bandit -c pyproject.toml -r backend/app, full backend pytest (coverage gate), and frontend pnpm lint + test + test:coverage + tsc. Fix ALL of them in your worktree on the same branch, then a single `fixup:` commit (do NOT amend, do NOT push — the orchestrator pushes).

Code findings:
1. **BLOCKING — migration revision-id collision**: your `b2c3d4e5f6a7` is already owned by the merged `b2c3d4e5f6a7_add_suite_notifications.py`. Generate a fresh unique revision id, rename the file to match, keep `down_revision="a1c2e3d4f5b6"`. Verify `conda run -n dataq python -m alembic heads` (from backend/) shows exactly ONE head and `alembic history` traverses clean. This is why the Playwright CI job is red.
2. **PATCH owner_user_id**: pre-check the User row exists → clean 422 (match the suite/share FK-precheck idiom), no unhandled IntegrityError.
3. **description cap**: `Field(max_length=1024)` on AssetMetadataUpdate (match SuiteCreate).
4. **GET /assets pagination**: limit/offset query params with the /runs idiom (`ge=1, le=200`, default 200 — check runs.py for exact shape) threaded into list_visible_assets.
5. **health.ts must consider run.status**: operational `failed` → an error/failing tag (not green); `queued`/`running` → don't show green "Passing" (use the run-status handling conventions from the Results/dashboard pages — read how they map status+severity and mirror it).
6. **AssetDetail: swap raw antd List → the SimpleList shim** (the #516/#693 component) for the lineage panels.
7. **updateAsset dead client**: wire a minimal admin-only edit for `description` on AssetDetail (useMe().is_workspace_admin gating pattern; small inline edit or modal; test it). Owner reassignment UI stays deferred — note that in code where natural.
8. **Orphan assets for admin**: `get_visible_asset(include_all=True)` must return suite-less assets (empty suites list) instead of 404; non-admin behavior unchanged. Test both.

Test gaps to close (from the test-quality angle):
a. 404-no-leak asserted on BODY equality (unknown id vs no-grant → identical status AND body).
b. PATCH tested through the real route with JSON bodies: explicit `null` vs omitted field semantics (model_fields_set), unknown fields rejected.
c. Malformed/garbage UUID on both GET endpoints (expect 4xx, not 500).
d. NUL-byte + oversized description via the route (the #570 guard class; expect 422).
e. Lineage BFS: cycle in lineage_edges + a node reachable both upstream and downstream (depth-cap respected, no hang).
f. Asset whose only composing suite is deleted after having runs (detail + list behavior).

Then run the FULL gate: black --check backend/, ruff check backend/, mypy backend/app backend/tests, bandit -c pyproject.toml -r backend/app, full backend pytest (coverage gate), and frontend pnpm lint + test + test:coverage + tsc. Also re-verify migration up/down/up against the alembic graph (not direct Operations binding — that's how the collision slipped past). content.text: Both CI failures are fixed and pushed (the CodeQL `side-effect-in-assert` restructured out of the no-leak test, and the Playwright selectors made exact-match so they don't collide with the empty-state text). Auto-merge stays armed; a watcher is on it. Once #772 lands I'll file the nav-inversion issue and launch the #770 sweep + #761 incidents builders.

This 'assert' statement contains an expression which may have side effects.
backend/tests/api/test_assets.py:250

That's the CodeQL alert I just fixed in `3a39922d` — the old code did `assert body["error"]["detail"].pop("asset_id") == …`, putting the mutating `.pop()` inside the assert. The restructured version does the `.pop()` inside `normalized()`, which is now called as a plain statement, and the asserts only compare the already-computed values:

```python
def normalized(resp: Any) -> tuple[str, dict[str, Any]]:
    body: dict[str, Any] = resp.json()
    echoed: str = body["error"]["detail"].pop("asset_id")
    return echoed, body

existing_echo, existing_body = normalized(existing)
unknown_echo, unknown_body = normalized(unknown)
assert existing_echo == str(world["asset_x"])
assert unknown_echo == str(unknown_id)
assert existing_body == unknown_body
```

No assert carries a side effect anymore (and the test would no longer silently skip its checks under `python -O` either, which is the real hazard behind that rule). The alert you're seeing is the analysis of the previous commit — CodeQL is re-running on the pushed head now and the alert should close when that pass completes. The merge watcher is running; if CodeQL still flags anything on the new head I'll see the failing check and address it before merge.

input.prompt:
Build GitHub issue #761 for DataQ: incident objects — lifecycle, dedup vs alerts, evidence-card payload, ownership routing (G-d phase 3). You are in an isolated git worktree of /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ — create branch `feature/issue-761-incidents` and commit there. Do NOT touch the user's running docker stack or the main checkout.

## Read first (binding)
1. `gh issue view 761 -R TheurgicDuke771/DataQ` — scope + AC.
2. docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md §3 + docs/adr/0034-*.md decision 4 — design of record (one open incident per (asset_id, check_id); reopen = NEW incident linked to prior; evidence card layers; ResultPublisher delivery; suite-owner→asset-owner routing).
3. Existing machinery you MUST reuse, not duplicate: backend/app/alerting/ (ResultPublisher seam, dedup #369/#386, snooze #370, render.py), backend/app/services/run_service.py (where results persist + alerts dispatch — find the seam where incidents hook in), the #420 upsert-race discipline (ON CONFLICT idiom), asset_view_service.py authz derivation (#760 — incidents visibility mirrors it), backend/app/lineage/edges.py downstream_assets (blast radius for the evidence card), migration discipline (see below).

## Scope
- **`incidents` table** (ONE new migration): anchored to (asset_id FK, check_id FK), lifecycle `open → acknowledged → resolved` with `resolved_by: user|auto`, actor + timestamp per transition, `occurrence_count`, `last_seen_at`, `evidence` JSONB snapshot, `prior_incident_id` self-FK for reopen chains. **MIGRATION DISCIPLINE (a revision-id collision just burned us in #772):** generate a FRESH unique revision id, chain off the CURRENT single head (verify with `cd backend && conda run -n dataq python -m alembic heads` — must be exactly one head before AND after your file exists), additive only, up/down/up tested through the real alembic graph on a scratch DB.
- **Lifecycle engine** (new incident_service): on a failing result for (asset, check) — open a new incident if none open (upsert race-safe per #420: unique partial index on open state + ON CONFLICT), else attach occurrence (count + last_seen_at + evidence refresh). On the first PASSING result for the pair: auto-resolve (resolved_by=auto), default on, per-suite configurable (extend the existing per-suite notification/config surface — find where suite-level toggles live; if it needs a column, fold into the SAME migration). Manual transitions via API always win (ack, resolve, and a resolved pair's next failure opens a NEW incident linked via prior_incident_id).
- **Evidence card (layer 1, deterministic, no LLM)**: assembled from existing data only — upstream pipeline run for the suite's trigger + delay-vs-history, `metric_value` trend (last N results), profile-diff failing-vs-last-passing IF cheaply available (skip gracefully otherwise), sibling checks' latest outcomes, downstream blast radius via downstream_assets. **PII: never include sample_failures content** — redaction discipline is absolute. Snapshot at open, refreshed per occurrence.
- **Delivery**: the existing ResultPublisher path gains the incident reference/card in its payload (alerts stay per-result with their dedup/snooze untouched — they now reference the open incident id). Routing: prefer assets.owner_user_id when set, fallback suite owner; a small recipient-resolution function, no new routing engine.
- **Read API + minimal UI**: `GET /api/v1/incidents` (filter by asset/suite/state) + `GET /incidents/{id}` + `POST /incidents/{id}/ack` + `/resolve` — visibility mirrors #760's grant-derived rules exactly (404-no-leak; workspace-admin sees all). Frontend: an Incidents section on AssetDetail (open incidents with state, occurrence count, ack/resolve actions permission-gated) — a full incidents page defers to #773 nav inversion; note that in code.
- **run_service hook**: incidents update INSIDE the run-completion transaction or via the established post-persist seam — study how alerting hooks in and mirror it; fail-soft (an incident engine bug must never fail the run).

## Requirements
- AC checklist on the issue is binding: lifecycle+dedup state machine unit-tested (open/ack/resolve/auto-resolve/reopen, occurrence attach, concurrent-failure no-duplicate via real DB race test if the #420 tests show the pattern); evidence card from existing data only, PII-redacted, snapshotted+refreshed; publisher payload carries the card; per-suite auto-resolve config honored; authz matrix mirrors asset rules (owner/edit/view/no-share/admin + 404-no-leak, body-asserted like test_assets.py does).
- ≥80% coverage on new modules; adversarial inputs (garbage UUIDs, NUL bytes in ack notes if any text field exists — cap + validate).
- Full gate: black --check backend/, ruff check backend/, mypy backend/app backend/tests, bandit -c pyproject.toml -r backend/app, full backend pytest; frontend pnpm lint + test + test:coverage + tsc if you touch frontend.
- Conventional commits, one functionality per commit where practical (migration+model / service / api / frontend). Do NOT push, do NOT open a PR.
- A sibling agent is building #770 (orphan-asset sweep — touches worker/tasks.py beat wiring + config.py only, no models/migrations). Its PR lands BEFORE yours; keep your worker/tasks.py + config.py changes minimal and self-contained so the rebase is trivial. Also: when you add the incidents table, remember #770's sweep will need an incidents-reference guard — if its code is already visible on main when you rebase, add the guard + test; otherwise leave a TODO comment referencing #770.

## Deliverable (final message)
Branch name; migration id + head verification output; the state machine described in 5 lines; endpoint list + authz semantics; evidence-card field list; how the publisher payload changed; files changed grouped; test inventory (state machine, race, authz, adversarial) + coverage; gate outputs; caveats/decisions.

content.text: Rewriting the sweep to be atomic per chunk, with a guard registry that the schema-introspection test enforces:

input.old_string: _ORPHAN_SWEEP_CHUNK = 500
input.new_string:
_ORPHAN_SWEEP_CHUNK = 500

# Reference guards for the orphan sweep (#770): every FK into ``assets.id`` must
# have a ``(table, column)`` row here. This registry drives BOTH the sweep's
# NOT-EXISTS predicate and the schema-introspection test
# (`test_asset_sweep.py::test_every_asset_fk_has_a_sweep_guard`), so a new FK —
# e.g. #761 ``incidents.asset_id`` — fails the build until its guard is added.
_SWEEP_REFERENCE_GUARDS: tuple[tuple[str, str], ...] = (
    ("suites", "asset_id"),
    ("runs", "asset_id"),
    ("lineage_edges", "upstream_asset_id"),
    ("lineage_edges", "downstream_asset_id"),
)


def _sweep_guard_clauses() -> list[Any]:
    """NOT-EXISTS clause per registered reference guard, built from metadata."""
    tables = Asset.metadata.tables
    return [
        ~exists().where(tables[table_name].c[column_name] == Asset.id)
        for table_name, column_name in _SWEEP_REFERENCE_GUARDS
    ]

**Reference guard — an explicit checklist, not a derived query.** `Suite.
    asset_id` / `Run.asset_id` are ``ON DELETE SET NULL`` (see their model
    docstrings), so the schema alone would happily let a referenced asset be
    deleted; the guard below is what actually protects them, enforced in the
    service, not by the FK. Every table that can hold an `assets.id` reference
    needs its own line here:

    - `suites.asset_id`
    - `runs.asset_id`
    - `lineage_edges.upstream_asset_id` / `lineage_edges.downstream_asset_id`
    - (**forward-compat, #761**) `incidents.asset_id`, once the incidents table
      lands — add ``~exists().where(Incident.asset_id == Asset.id)`` to the
      ``where(...)`` clause below. A missing guard here is a silent over-delete,
      not a constraint violation, so treat this list as a checklist on every PR
      that adds a new `assets.id` foreign key.

    Selects candidate ids first, then deletes them in ``chunk_size`` batches (a
    large sweep — e.g. after a bulk lineage-source removal — never holds one
    giant DELETE open). A candidate referenced by a brand-new row between the
    select and its chunk's delete is a benign, vanishingly-rare race: the next
    tick simply won't re-select it once the reference exists at that point in
    time; there is no window where a *currently*-referenced asset is removed,
    since a delete only ever removes ids captured in an already-unreferenced
    snapshot. Returns the number of assets actually swept.
    """
    if retention_days <= 0:
        return 0
    moment = now or _now()
    cutoff = moment - timedelta(days=retention_days)

    candidate_ids = list(
        session.scalars(
            select(Asset.id).where(
                Asset.last_seen < cutoff,
                ~exists().where(Suite.asset_id == Asset.id),
                ~exists().where(Run.asset_id == Asset.id),
                ~exists().where(LineageEdge.upstream_asset_id == Asset.id),
                ~exists().where(LineageEdge.downstream_asset_id == Asset.id),
                # (#761 forward-compat) add here once `incidents` lands:
                #   ~exists().where(Incident.asset_id == Asset.id),
            )
        )
    )

    swept = 0
    for start in range(0, len(candidate_ids), chunk_size):
        chunk = candidate_ids[start : start + chunk_size]
        # session.execute(<DML>) returns a CursorResult; the typed overload widens
        # it to Result (no rowcount), so cast to read the affected-row count —
        # same pattern as `run_service.purge_expired_sample_failures`.
        result = cast(
            CursorResult[Any],
            session.execute(delete(Asset).where(Asset.id.in_(chunk))),
        )
        swept += result.rowcount or 0
    session.commit()
input.new_string:
**Reference guard — an enforced registry, not a checklist.** `Suite.
    asset_id` / `Run.asset_id` are ``ON DELETE SET NULL`` and the lineage-edge
    FKs are ``ON DELETE CASCADE``, so the schema alone would happily let a
    referenced asset be deleted (or its edges be cascade-wiped); the
    ``_SWEEP_REFERENCE_GUARDS`` registry is what actually protects them. Every
    FK into ``assets.id`` needs a registry row — the schema-introspection test
    (``test_every_asset_fk_has_a_sweep_guard``) fails the build when a new FK
    (e.g. #761 ``incidents.asset_id``) lands without one, so a silent
    over-delete cannot ship unnoticed.

    Deletes run as chunked ``DELETE … WHERE id IN (SELECT … LIMIT n)``
    statements that carry the FULL predicate (staleness + every guard), each
    committed before the next — a large sweep (e.g. after a bulk lineage-source
    removal) never holds one giant DELETE or a sweep-long transaction open, and
    a candidate that gains a reference mid-sweep is re-checked by its own chunk's
    subquery rather than deleted from a stale snapshot. The residual race is a
    single statement wide (a reference committed in the same instant a ≥30-day
    stale asset's chunk deletes it); its blast radius is bounded — suites/runs
    go ``SET NULL`` and the next save/refresh re-creates the asset row via the
    normal upsert path. Returns the number of assets actually swept.
    """
    if retention_days <= 0:
        return 0
    moment = now or _now()
    cutoff = moment - timedelta(days=retention_days)

    swept = 0
    while True:
        candidate_chunk = (
            select(Asset.id)
            .where(Asset.last_seen < cutoff, *_sweep_guard_clauses())
            .limit(chunk_size)
            .scalar_subquery()
        )
        # session.execute(<DML>) returns a CursorResult; the typed overload widens
        # it to Result (no rowcount), so cast to read the affected-row count —
        # same pattern as `run_service.purge_expired_sample_failures`.
        result = cast(
            CursorResult[Any],
            session.execute(delete(Asset).where(Asset.id.in_(candidate_chunk))),
        )
        deleted = result.rowcount or 0
        session.commit()
        swept += deleted
        if deleted < chunk_size:
            break

def test_every_asset_fk_has_a_sweep_guard() -> None:
    """Schema-introspection enforcement of `_SWEEP_REFERENCE_GUARDS` (#770).

    Any FK into ``assets.id`` (e.g. #761's ``incidents.asset_id``) must carry a
    sweep guard, or the janitor silently over-deletes referenced assets. This
    test turns that from a code-comment checklist into a build failure.
    """
    from backend.app.db.models import Asset
    from backend.app.services.asset_service import _SWEEP_REFERENCE_GUARDS

    fk_refs = {
        (fk.parent.table.name, fk.parent.name)
        for table in Asset.metadata.tables.values()
        for fk in table.foreign_keys
        if fk.column.table.name == "assets" and fk.column.name == "id"
    }
    assert fk_refs == set(_SWEEP_REFERENCE_GUARDS)
EOF

<result>All checks green. The work is complete on branch `feature/issue-761-incidents` (7 commits, clean tree, not pushed).

## Summary — Issue #761: incident objects (G-d phase 3)

**Branch:** `feature/issue-761-incidents` (off `main` @ `a4093c04`). Not pushed, no PR.

**Migration:** `c4e5a6b7d8f9` (add_incidents), chained off `f0a1b2c3d4e5`. Single head verified before AND after (`alembic heads` → `c4e5a6b7d8f9 (head)`); up→down→up green through the real alembic graph on a scratch DB (table + partial unique index + `auto_resolve_incidents` default all confirmed). Additive only.

**State machine (5 lines):**
1. A breaching result (warn/fail/critical) opens a new incident for `(asset_id, check_id)` via `INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` on the partial unique index (`active = open|acknowledged`).
2. On conflict it falls through to attach: `occurrence_count++`, `last_seen_at` + evidence refreshed — never a duplicate (upsert-race-safe, #420).
3. The first passing result auto-resolves the active incident (`resolved_by=auto`), default-on per-suite `auto_resolve_incidents` toggle; `skip`/`error` are no-ops.
4. Manual `ack` (open→acknowledged) and `resolve` (→resolved, `resolved_by=user`) always win; a resolved row is never re-mutated (409 on double-resolve / ack-of-resolved).
5. A resolved pair's next breach opens a NEW incident linked via `prior_incident_id` (reopen chain).

**Endpoints + authz:** `GET /api/v1/incidents` (filter asset/suite/state) · `GET /incidents/{id}` (+evidence) · `POST /incidents/{id}/ack` · `/resolve`. Visibility derives from suite grants (`accessible_suite_ids`, same as #760); read=`view`, act=`edit`; 404-no-leak (unknown and ungranted return byte-identical bodies); workspace-admin sees/acts everywhere.

**Evidence card (layer 1, deterministic, existing-data-only, PII-safe):** `generated_at`, `check`, `asset`, `failing_result{status,metric_value,observed_value,expected_value}` (**never `sample_failures`**), `metric_trend` (last 10 readings), `sibling_checks`, `upstream_pipeline_run{…,duration_seconds,delay_seconds_vs_history}` (via `triggered_by` correlation, null for manual), `downstream_blast_radius` (lineage BFS), `profile_diff: null` (documented — needs live introspection). Snapshotted at open, refreshed per occurrence.

**Publisher payload change:** `RunReport` gained `incidents: list[IncidentCard]` (default `[]`; `IncidentCard{incident_id, check_id, check_name, status, occurrence_count, is_new, evidence}`). `build_run_report` populates one card per breaching check with an active incident; the fail-soft `sync_incidents_for_run` hook runs in `run_suite` **before** alert dispatch, so alerts stay per-result but reference the open incident.

**Files changed (grouped):**
- migration+model: `alembic/versions/c4e5a6b7d8f9_add_incidents.py`, `app/db/models.py`
- service: `app/services/incident_service.py`, `app/services/incident_evidence.py`, `app/worker/tasks.py`
- alerting: `app/alerting/base.py`, `app/alerting/builder.py`
- api: `app/api/v1/incidents.py`, `app/main.py`
- frontend: `src/api/incidents.ts`, `src/components/assets/IncidentsPanel.tsx`, `src/pages/AssetDetail.tsx`
- tests: `tests/services/test_incident_service.py`, `test_incident_evidence.py`, `tests/api/test_incidents.py`, `tests/alerting/test_incident_payload.py`, `tests/db/test_incidents_migration.py`; frontend `tests/components/IncidentsPanel.test.tsx`, `tests/api/incidents.test.ts`, `tests/pages/AssetDetail.test.tsx`
- docs: `docs/architecture.md` (ER diagram), `docs/progress.md`

**Test inventory (49 backend + 13 new frontend):** state machine 18 (open/warn/error+skip/no-asset/operational-failure/attach/auto-resolve/config-off/default-on/reopen/ack→resolve/ack-still-dedups/double-resolve-409/ack-resolved-409/list-filters/active-map); race 2 (deterministic ON-CONFLICT fallback **+ a genuine two-connection concurrent race** proving exactly one active incident, count=2); evidence 8 (incl. the PII floor — a `sample_failures`-stuffed result yields a card with the secret nowhere); authz+adversarial 18 (full owner/edit/view/no-share/admin matrix, 404-no-leak body-asserted, garbage-UUID/NUL/over-cap-note 422); payload 3; migration 2. Frontend: IncidentsPanel 8 + client 5.

**Coverage (new modules):** `incident_service` 96%, `incident_evidence` 92%, `api/v1/incidents` 99%; frontend `incidents.ts` 100%, `IncidentsPanel.tsx` 96.9% lines — all ≥80%.

**Gate:** `black --check` (234 files) ✓ · `ruff` ✓ · `mypy app tests` (234 files, no issues) ✓ · `bandit` ✓ · 49 incident tests pass · regression areas (alerting/worker/run_service/assets/db) 272+ pass (ER-diagram drift guard fixed by adding the `incidents` entity) · full frontend suite 443 pass · prettier ✓.

**Caveats / decisions:**
- **#770 guard:** its orphan-asset-sweep code isn't on `main` yet, so per instructions I left a `TODO(#770)` in `incident_service` noting the sweep must add an `EXISTS (open|acknowledged incidents)` guard (incidents CASCADE from asset). No worker/tasks.py beat or config.py changes were needed (the incident hook is inline in `run_suite`), keeping the eventual rebase trivial.
- Operational run failures (no result rows) and `error`/`skip` results open **no** incident — there's no check-level anchor for `(asset,check)`; they still alert via existing paths.
- ack/resolve gated on `edit` (an operational action, like triggering a run); read on `view`. I read "mirror asset rules" as the derived-visibility + 404-no-leak matrix, not asset-metadata's admin-only mutation.
- Per-suite auto-resolve toggle folded into the same migration (`suite_notifications.auto_resolve_incidents`, default true).</result>

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>" && git push -qu origin feature/issue-761-incidents && gh pr create --head feature/issue-761-incidents --title "feat(incidents): incident objects — lifecycle, dedup, evidence card, publisher payload, grant-derived API (G-d phase 3)" --milestone "v1.1 Week 3 — Azure wind-down + local-first posture" --body "Closes #761

G-d phase 3 (design: docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md §3, ADR 0034 decision 4).

## What
- **\`incidents\` table** (migration \`c4e5a6b7d8f9\`, chained off \`f0a1b2c3d4e5\`, single head verified up/down/up through the real alembic graph): anchored to \`(asset_id, check_id)\`, lifecycle \`open → acknowledged → resolved\` with actor+timestamp per transition, \`occurrence_count\`/\`last_seen_at\`, \`evidence\` JSONB, \`prior_incident_id\` reopen chain; **partial unique index = at most one active incident per pair**.
- **Lifecycle engine** (\`incident_service\`): breaching result → \`INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING\` (the #420 race discipline — proven by a genuine two-connection concurrent race test) or occurrence attach; first passing result auto-resolves (\`resolved_by=auto\`, per-suite \`auto_resolve_incidents\` toggle, default on, same migration); manual ack/resolve always win (409 on re-mutation of resolved); resolved pair's next breach opens a NEW linked incident. \`skip\`/\`error\`/operational failures open nothing (no per-check anchor) — they still alert via existing paths.
- **Evidence card** (layer 1, deterministic, existing-data-only): failing result (status/metric/observed/expected — **never \`sample_failures\`**, PII floor tested), metric trend (last 10), sibling checks, upstream pipeline run + delay-vs-history via \`triggered_by\`, downstream blast radius via the lineage BFS. Snapshotted at open, refreshed per occurrence.
- **Publisher payload**: \`RunReport.incidents\` (list of \`IncidentCard\`) populated by the fail-soft \`sync_incidents_for_run\` hook in \`run_suite\` before alert dispatch — alerts stay per-result (dedup/snooze untouched) and now reference the open incident.
- **API + UI**: \`GET /incidents\` (asset/suite/state filters) · \`GET /incidents/{id}\` · \`POST …/ack\` · \`…/resolve\`; visibility grant-derived like #760 (read=view, act=edit, 404-no-leak body-identical, admin everywhere). Incidents panel on AssetDetail (permission-gated actions); the full incidents page defers to #773.
- **#770 integration** (added on rebase): \`incidents.asset_id\` registered in \`_SWEEP_REFERENCE_GUARDS\` — the schema-introspection test failed on rebase exactly as designed, and incident history now pins its asset (the FK is CASCADE).

## Tests
49 backend incident tests (state machine 18 · race 2 incl. a real two-connection race · evidence 8 incl. the PII floor · authz+adversarial 18 · payload 3 · migration 2) + the sweep guard test + 13 frontend. Coverage: \`incident_service\` 96%, \`incident_evidence\` 92%, \`api/v1/incidents\` 99%, frontend new modules ≥96%.

## Gate (post-rebase on #774)
Backend **1868 passed, 98.25%**; frontend **443 passed**, tsc/lint clean; black/ruff/mypy/bandit clean; alembic single head, up/down/up green.

input.message:
The 5-angle review of your PR (#775) is done — migration angle fully clean, but there's a fix batch. Six findings are inline on the PR (`gh api repos/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pulls/775/comments`); plus cross-file/test items below. Fix ALL in your worktree (already rebased on main — note `_SWEEP_REFERENCE_GUARDS` now includes incidents and there's a new sweep test), single `fixup:` commit, do NOT push.

Inline findings:
1. **[high] ack/resolve lost-update race**: no row lock → concurrent auto-resolve then manual ack reopens a terminal row; can 500 on the partial unique index. `with_for_update()` + post-lock status re-check in both `acknowledge_incident`/`resolve_incident`; add a two-session barrier race test (copy your open/attach pattern).
2. **[high] ON-CONFLICT fallback None crash**: `_active_incident` None after conflict (resolved-in-gap) raises and rolls back the WHOLE run's sync. Retry the open (the pair is free now) — bounded loop, not recursion.
3. **[high] evidence layers unwrapped**: each layer must be try/except → None + `log.warning`, making the best-effort docstring true. Test: a monkeypatched raising layer still yields a card (other layers intact) and never poisons the sync.
4. **[medium/PII] card `observed_value`**: strip list-valued sample-bearing keys (`partial_unexpected_list`, `unexpected_index_list` — reuse/reference the `_SAMPLE_KEYS` notion from gx_runner) before embedding; correct the docstring (scalars are sanctioned post-#416, lists are not). Test with a stuffed result.
5. **[medium] `GET /incidents`**: add `offset` (ge=0) mirroring the #772 /assets fix + truncation test beyond the cap.
6. **[low] `is_new`**: use the engine's actual open-vs-attach outcome rather than `occurrence_count == 1` at alert-build time (or created_at == last_seen_at comparison — pick the cleanest given your sync flow, and say which).

Cross-file + test items:
7. **Channels never render incidents**: RunReport carries the cards but Slack/email/Teams templates ignore them — users never see incident info. Add a MINIMAL incident line via the shared `alerting/render.py` (e.g. "Incident #<short-id> <status>, occurrence N" per failing check that has one) so Slack + email get it, and one Teams card fact; deep formatting can defer to the nav-inversion phase (note it). Test at the render layer.
8. **Frontend**: IncidentsPanel reinvents a severity color map — reuse `RESULT_STATUS_COLORS`/the shared map from `resultsFormat.ts`. Also add the untested ack/resolve FAILURE branch test (message.error + busy reset + no reload).
9. **Demo seed**: `scripts/demo_data.py` seeds failing results but never calls `sync_incidents_for_run` → empty Incidents panel on a fresh stack. Seed at least one open incident through the real sync call.
10. **Fail-soft proof**: add the injected-failure test for `sync_incidents_for_run` (monkeypatch the engine to raise; run persists + alerts still dispatch) — mirror `test_publisher_exception_is_swallowed`.
11. Evaluate and fix-or-refute with a sentence each: (a) cancelled-run handling in the sync hook; (b) check-delete → incidents CASCADE behavior (add a test either way — the #540 suite-delete lesson); (c) the claimed `suite_id` vs `(asset_id, check_id)` dedup-key mismatch in `active_incidents_for_run` for shared-asset scenarios.

Then FULL gate again (backend black/ruff/mypy/bandit/pytest with coverage; frontend lint/test/tsc). Report per-finding what changed, new test names, gate outputs.

6. **[low] `is_new`**: use the engine's actual open-vs-attach outcome rather than `occurrence_count == 1` at alert-build time (or created_at == last_seen_at comparison — pick the cleanest given your sync flow, and say which).

Cross-file + test items:
7. **Channels never render incidents**: RunReport carries the cards but Slack/email/Teams templates ignore them — users never see incident info. Add a MINIMAL incident line via the shared `alerting/render.py` (e.g. "Incident #<short-id> <status>, occurrence N" per failing check that has one) so Slack + email get it, and one Teams card fact; deep formatting can defer to the nav-inversion phase (note it). Test at the render layer.
8. **Frontend**: IncidentsPanel reinvents a severity color map — reuse `RESULT_STATUS_COLORS`/the shared map from `resultsFormat.ts`. Also add the untested ack/resolve FAILURE branch test (message.error + busy reset + no reload).
9. **Demo seed**: `scripts/demo_data.py` seeds failing results but never calls `sync_incidents_for_run` → empty Incidents panel on a fresh stack. Seed at least one open incident through the real sync call.
10. **Fail-soft proof**: add the injected-failure test for `sync_incidents_for_run` (monkeypatch the engine to raise; run persists + alerts still dispatch) — mirror `test_publisher_exception_is_swallowed`.
11. Evaluate and fix-or-refute with a sentence each: (a) cancelled-run handling in the sync hook; (b) check-delete → incidents CASCADE behavior (add a test either way — the #540 suite-delete lesson); (c) the claimed `suite_id` vs `(asset_id, check_id)` dedup-key mismatch in `active_incidents_for_run` for shared-asset scenarios.

Then FULL gate again (backend black/ruff/mypy/bandit/pytest with coverage; frontend lint/test/tsc). Report per-finding what changed, new test names, gate outputs.

Existing machinery to mirror/reuse: backend/app/lineage/edges.py (refresh_dbt_edges — the upsert/prune pattern, `source` scoping, connection provenance), backend/app/lineage/emitter.py + dispatch.py (#758 — Settings-gated client, fail-open), backend/app/services/asset_identity.py (OL naming — identity join with Marquez is free), backend/app/orchestration/base.py (the OrchestrationProvider seam discipline — yours mirrors it: no provider-specific branching in service code), docker-compose.yml (the local stack; scratchpad had a marquez-compose from the #758 session but build the in-repo one fresh and canonical).

## Scope (from the issue)
- **`LineageProvider` abstraction** (backend/app/lineage/provider.py or similar): `get_lineage(asset, depth) -> graph` mirroring the OrchestrationProvider discipline. The graph's node type must carry a **node kind** seam (dataset today; job/BI-report/dashboard reserved — the #762-comment contract: downstream nodes aren't always tables), plus OL namespace/name.
- **Marquez reference impl**: `GET {base}/api/v1/lineage?nodeId=dataset:{ns}:{name}&depth=N` (Marquez 0.50.0 API — verify the exact param/response shape from Marquez docs or the openlineage client if vendored knowledge is uncertain; the #758 session verified a live Marquez query API works). Config: where does the Marquez URL live? Follow the emitter's Settings pattern (e.g. `LINEAGE_PROVIDER=marquez` + `MARQUEZ_URL`, dark by default — document in .env.app.example / deploy example like OPENLINEAGE_URL was). Timeouts + fail-soft like the emitter (5s, no-latch).

- **Refresh trigger**: a periodic pull (beat task following the janitor/poll patterns, dark unless configured) — and/or on-demand; pick the design that mirrors ADR 0034's "pulled, never built" posture and say why.
- **In-repo compose Marquez**: add Marquez (2 containers: marquez + its postgres, reusing the repo's postgres image/pattern) to docker-compose.yml as an OPT-IN profile (`--profile lineage` or similar — must NOT start by default and must not break `docker compose up` for existing users). Record the stalled-release risk note (0.50.0, 2024-10) where ADR 0034 says.
- **Deferred behind the seam (do NOT build)**: DataHub / OpenMetadata (license-blocked, ADR 0031) / Purview — the seam's docstring lists them.
- **Contract tests** (mocked HTTP — respx or the repo's http-mock pattern; NO live Marquez in CI) + **adversarial payload battery** (tests/support/adversarial.py style): garbage JSON, huge graphs (cap/depth guard), cyclic graphs, unknown node kinds (must parse, not crash — the node-kind seam), missing namespaces, non-dataset nodeIds. Round-trip test: emitter→Marquez→pull is the compose story — cover the pull-parse-upsert path with a canned real-shaped Marquez response fixture; the LIVE round-trip is a manual/compose verification, not CI (document how to run it in the docs page you touch — docs/orchestration.md has the lineage section; add a short provider subsection or a new docs page, wherever it naturally extends).
- Update ADR 0034 "Related"/status ONLY if scope shifted (per AC).

## Requirements
- ≥80% coverage on new modules; black/ruff/mypy(app+tests)/bandit + full backend pytest green; conventional commits; do NOT push or open a PR.
- No new deps if avoidable (plain httpx/requests — check what the repo already uses for HTTP; the emitter uses openlineage-python's transport — the PULL should use the repo's existing HTTP client library, not a new one). License rule: nothing strong-copyleft.
